title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: A national energy advisory body has asked you to prepare a report on public attitudes towards nuclear energy in your country. Your report should summarise the main concerns and areas of support identified among the public, assess the factors that most strongly influence opinion, and recommend communication strategies that could contribute to a more informed national debate.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on Public Attitudes Towards Nuclear Energy**

**Introduction**
This report summarises findings from a national survey and focus group programme examining public attitudes towards nuclear energy. Its purpose is to identify the principal concerns and areas of support, assess the factors most strongly influencing opinion, and recommend strategies to improve the quality of public debate. A total of four thousand two hundred respondents participated across six regional cohorts.

**Principal Concerns**
Safety and accident risk emerged as the most frequently cited concern, referenced by sixty-eight percent of respondents. Long-term radioactive waste disposal was the second most prevalent issue, particularly among participants aged under thirty-five. A significant minority expressed concern about construction costs and their implications for household energy bills.

**Areas of Support**
Approximately forty-four percent of respondents viewed nuclear power positively as a low-carbon energy source capable of complementing renewables. Support was notably higher among those with science or engineering educational backgrounds and those living in regions with existing nuclear infrastructure.

**Factors Influencing Opinion**
Exposure to media coverage of historical accidents was the strongest predictor of negative opinion. Respondents who reported having received formal science education held measurably more nuanced views, suggesting that knowledge-based engagement moderates fear-driven responses.

**Recommendations**
Three measures are recommended: commissioning independent, accessible public information resources separating current reactor technology from historical accident contexts; facilitating community dialogue programmes in regions near proposed sites; and requiring clearer public reporting on waste management progress.

**Conclusion**
Improving the quality of debate requires treating the public as capable of engaging with complexity, provided information is accurate, accessible and institutionally credible.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt: You have read a proposal in your local newspaper suggesting that a new nuclear power station should be built in your region to support the national energy transition. Write a formal letter to your regional councillor expressing your views on the proposal, identifying both potential benefits and concerns, and requesting that a thorough public consultation be conducted before any decision is made.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Councillor Harrington,

I am writing in response to the proposal reported in the Westfield Gazette on 14 March suggesting that a new nuclear power station be constructed at the Dunmore coastal site as part of the national energy transition programme. I write neither as an unconditional supporter nor as an outright opponent of the proposal, but as a resident who believes that a decision of this magnitude requires considerably more public scrutiny than has thus far been undertaken.

I recognise that there are genuine arguments in favour of the development. Nuclear generation offers reliable, low-carbon electricity that could meaningfully support the region's capacity as intermittent renewable sources are expanded, and the economic benefits in terms of construction employment and long-term operational roles are not trivial in an area with persistently above-average unemployment.

Nevertheless, I have significant concerns. The management and long-term storage of radioactive waste produced at the site has not been addressed in any public document I have been able to locate. Furthermore, the track record of recent nuclear construction projects across Europe — at Hinkley Point C in the United Kingdom and Flamanville in France in particular — raises serious questions about cost overruns and timeline delays that would ultimately fall upon local and national taxpayers.

I would therefore respectfully request that your office call for a formal and independently facilitated public consultation process before any planning consent is sought, ensuring that residents are provided with full environmental impact assessments and transparent cost projections.

Yours sincerely,
Margaret Forsyth
14 Craigmoor Terrace, Westfield

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently watched a documentary called 'Power and Risk: The Nuclear Question' which examines the history of nuclear energy, profiles current reactor technologies, and debates whether nuclear power should be expanded as part of the global response to climate change. Write a review for a science and society magazine, evaluating the documentary's balance, the quality of expert testimony it presents, and whether it advances public understanding of a complex issue.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Balanced, Rigorous and Timely: A Review of 'Power and Risk: The Nuclear Question'**

Few energy topics provoke as much visceral public disagreement as nuclear power, and documentaries addressing it have a poor track record of resisting the temptation to confirm rather than challenge existing convictions. 'Power and Risk: The Nuclear Question', directed by Carola Henriksen and produced by Nordic Science Films, is a welcome exception.

The documentary covers three broad territories: the history of commercial nuclear power from the 1950s through the major accidents at Chernobyl and Fukushima; the landscape of current and emerging reactor technologies, including small modular reactors and Generation IV designs; and the contemporary policy debate about nuclear's role in decarbonisation. Henriksen devotes roughly equal screen time to each, and the pacing is measured enough that viewers are never made to feel rushed through difficult material.

The expert testimony is the film's greatest strength. Physicists, environmental scientists, economists, former plant operators and anti-nuclear campaigners are all given substantive time to articulate their positions without being reduced to soundbites. The documentary does not pretend that these positions are easily reconciled, and it is all the more intellectually honest for that.

If there is a weakness, it is that the human stories of communities affected by nuclear accidents, while present, feel somewhat compressed relative to the technical analysis.

Overall, 'Power and Risk' is precisely the kind of rigorous, good-faith public science communication the nuclear debate urgently needs. It is highly recommended viewing for policymakers, educators and engaged citizens alike.
